questions.stem: In the sentence "The teacher praised the diligent student," what part of speech is the word "diligent"?
questions.explanation: An adjective modifies a noun or pronoun by describing a quality. Here "diligent" tells us what kind of student is meant, so it modifies the noun "student" and is an adjective. It is not a noun (it does not name the person), not a verb (it shows no action or state), and not an adverb (it does not modify a verb, adjective, or other adverb).
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Identify the verb in this sentence: "The children quickly finished their homework."
questions.explanation: A verb expresses an action or a state of being. "Finished" names the action the children performed, so it is the verb. "Children" and "homework" are nouns, and "quickly" is an adverb telling how the action was done.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: In "She sings beautifully," what part of speech is the word "beautifully"?
questions.explanation: An adverb modifies a verb, an adjective, or another adverb, often telling how, when, or where. "Beautifully" tells how she sings, so it modifies the verb "sings" and is an adverb. The matching adjective would be "beautiful," which describes a noun instead.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: What part of speech is the underlined word in "The book is on the table"? The underlined word is "on".
questions.explanation: A preposition shows the relationship between a noun or pronoun and another word in the sentence, often relating to place or time. "On" links "table" to "book" and shows position, so it is a preposition. It begins the prepositional phrase "on the table."
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Identify the conjunction in "I wanted to leave, but the rain had not stopped."
questions.explanation: A conjunction joins words, phrases, or clauses. "But" is a coordinating conjunction that links the two independent clauses while showing contrast between them. The other options are a verb, a noun, and a verb respectively.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: In "They gave it to him," how many pronouns are present?
questions.explanation: A pronoun stands in place of a noun. "They," "it," and "him" all replace nouns, giving three pronouns. "They" is a subject pronoun, "it" is an object pronoun standing for a thing, and "him" is an object pronoun standing for a person.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: What part of speech is "water" in the sentence "They water the garden every morning"?
questions.explanation: Word class depends on the job a word does in its sentence. Although "water" is often a noun, here it names the action the subject performs on the garden, so it functions as a verb. This shows that the same word can belong to different classes depending on use.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Identify the interjection in "Ouch! That really hurt."
questions.explanation: An interjection is a word or short phrase that expresses sudden emotion and stands apart grammatically from the rest of the sentence. "Ouch" expresses pain and is followed by an exclamation mark, marking it as an interjection. The remaining words form a normal statement.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: In "This is my pen, not yours," what part of speech is the word "my"?
questions.explanation: A possessive adjective comes before a noun and shows ownership of it. "My" sits before the noun "pen" and tells whose pen it is, so it is a possessive adjective (also called a possessive determiner). By contrast "yours" stands alone in place of a noun and is a possessive pronoun.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which word in "The very tall man entered" is an adverb?
questions.explanation: An adverb can modify an adjective as well as a verb. "Very" modifies the adjective "tall" by telling the degree of tallness, so it is an adverb. "Tall" is an adjective describing "man," "man" is a noun, and "entered" is a verb.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Identify the part of speech of "because" in "He stayed home because he was ill."
questions.explanation: A subordinating conjunction joins a dependent clause to a main clause and shows the relationship between them. "Because" introduces the clause "he was ill" and links it to the main clause to give a reason, so it is a conjunction.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: In "Honesty is a great virtue," what kind of noun is "honesty"?
questions.explanation: An abstract noun names something that cannot be perceived by the five senses, such as a quality, idea, or feeling. "Honesty" names a quality that cannot be touched or seen, so it is an abstract noun. A concrete noun would name a physical thing, and a proper noun would name a specific person or place with a capital letter.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: What part of speech is "and" in "bread and butter"?
questions.explanation: A coordinating conjunction joins words or groups of equal grammatical rank. "And" links the two nouns "bread" and "butter," so it is a conjunction. It is not a preposition, since it shows no relationship of place or time, and not an article.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Identify the article in "She bought an umbrella."
questions.explanation: An article is a special kind of determiner that marks a noun as definite or indefinite. "An" is the indefinite article used before a word beginning with a vowel sound, and it introduces the noun "umbrella." The articles in English are "a," "an," and "the."
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: In "Run fast or you will miss the bus," what part of speech is "fast"?
questions.explanation: Word class is decided by function. Here "fast" tells how to run, so it modifies the verb "run" and is an adverb. The word "fast" can also be an adjective, as in "a fast car," but in this sentence its job is to modify a verb.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: What part of speech is "ourselves" in "We blamed ourselves for the loss"?
questions.explanation: A reflexive pronoun ends in "self" or "selves" and refers back to the subject of the same clause. "Ourselves" refers back to the subject "we," showing that the doers and the receivers of the action are the same, so it is a reflexive pronoun.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Identify the preposition in "The cat hid under the bed."
questions.explanation: A preposition relates a noun or pronoun to another word and often shows location. "Under" shows the position of the cat relative to the bed, so it is a preposition that begins the phrase "under the bed." "Hid" is a verb and "cat" and "bed" are nouns.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: In "The committee has reached its decision," what type of noun is "committee"?
questions.explanation: A collective noun names a group of people or things considered as one unit. "Committee" refers to a body of people acting together, so it is a collective noun. This is also why it takes the singular verb "has" when treated as a single unit.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: What part of speech is "which" in "The house, which stood on the hill, was old"?
questions.explanation: A relative pronoun introduces a relative clause and refers back to a noun mentioned earlier. "Which" introduces the clause "which stood on the hill" and refers back to "house," so it is a relative pronoun. Other relative pronouns include "who," "whom," "whose," and "that."
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Identify the adjective in "Three brave soldiers guarded the gate."
questions.explanation: An adjective describes a quality of a noun. "Brave" describes the quality of the soldiers, so it is an adjective. "Three" is a number that acts as a determiner here, "soldiers" is a noun, and "guarded" is a verb. Of the options, "brave" is the clearest descriptive adjective of quality.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: In "Wow, that is amazing news!" what part of speech is "Wow"?
questions.explanation: An interjection expresses a sudden feeling and is grammatically independent from the sentence around it. "Wow" expresses surprise or delight and could be removed without breaking the sentence, so it is an interjection.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: What part of speech is "slowly" in "The slowly moving train left the station"?
questions.explanation: An adverb can modify an adjective or a participle as well as a plain verb. "Slowly" modifies the participle "moving" by telling how the train was moving, so it is an adverb. The clue is the "ly" ending and the fact that it answers the question how.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Identify the demonstrative pronoun in "These are the keys I lost."
questions.explanation: A demonstrative pronoun points to a specific thing and stands in place of a noun. "These" stands alone as the subject and points to the items being shown, so it is a demonstrative pronoun. The demonstratives are "this," "that," "these," and "those."
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: What part of speech is "however" in "The plan was risky; however, they approved it"?
questions.explanation: A conjunctive adverb links two independent clauses and shows a logical relationship such as contrast, while still behaving like an adverb. "However" connects the two clauses and signals contrast, and it is typically set off by a semicolon before it and a comma after it, marking it as a conjunctive adverb rather than a simple coordinating conjunction.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide
